title: Pao de queijo
subtitle: Brazilian cheese bread bites, gluten-free and crisp outside
excerpt: Pao de queijo are small, glossy cheese bread rolls made with tapioca flour rather than wheat. They are naturally gluten-free. The crust is crisp and slightly chewy; the inside is hollow and airy. Eaten warm, they are exceptional. Cold, they are less so.
sourceNotes: Pao de queijo is a Brazilian cheese bread with origins in the state of Minas Gerais, where dairy production has been central to the economy since the eighteenth century. The use of tapioca flour (polvilho) as the base reflects the indigenous Amazonian tradition of cassava processing. The recipe spread with Brazilian migration and is now served across Brazil at any time of day, from street stalls to airline breakfasts. This recipe follows the established domestic method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Shape the dough into balls, freeze on a tray until solid, then transfer to a bag. Bake from frozen at 200 degrees C for 28 to 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles cleanly. Mix in a larger bowl; bake on two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be shaped into balls and refrigerated for up to 24 hours before baking. Bake directly from the fridge.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the rolls puff and crisp in 20 to 25 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: When hot liquid is poured over tapioca flour and mixed, the starch granules absorb the liquid and swell, forming a stretchy, slightly translucent gel. This gel is the structure of pao de queijo: it gives the rolls their characteristic chew and their hollow interior.

content.text: The technique in pao de queijo is simple but unfamiliar to anyone who has only worked with wheat flour. Hot liquid is poured over tapioca flour and beaten until the starch forms a sticky, elastic
. Eggs and cheese go in, and the result shapes into rough balls with wet hands. Bake hot and eat immediately.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays. Put 250 g tapioca flour and 5 g salt in a large bowl. Combine 120 ml whole milk and 60 ml olive oil in a small saucepan and bring to the boil. Pour the hot liquid over the tapioca flour at once. Mix quickly with a wooden spoon until the flour absorbs all the liquid and forms a lumpy, sticky dough. Leave to cool for 10 minutes until warm rather than hot.

Beat 2 eggs into the cooled dough, a little at a time, mixing well after each addition. The dough will look broken at first; keep mixing and it will smooth out. Stir in 100 g grated parmesan. The finished dough should be soft, sticky and a little shiny. It will not look like bread dough.

Wet your hands lightly with water. Scoop approximately 25 g portions of dough and roll briefly between your palms into rough balls. Place on the lined trays with 3 cm between each. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until puffed, golden and crisp. They should sound hollow when tapped on the base. Eat immediately while the crust is crisp; the rolls soften as they cool.

items.problem: Rolls come out flat rather than puffed
items.solution: The milk and oil mixture was not hot enough when added to the tapioca, so the starch did not gelatinise properly. It must be a proper boil, not just warm. Or the dough was too wet; scoop slightly smaller portions next time.

items.problem: Dough is impossible to shape into balls
items.solution: The dough is meant to be very sticky. Wet your hands with cold water rather than flouring them. Work quickly and do not try to smooth the balls perfectly.
